The Profit Center 	7_Q_and_A 	Page 1 of 32
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 1 of 32
Bob Bly: 	So this is – welcome everyone to the bonus, bonus call. Last week
was the bonus call but we didn’t get through all the questions. So,
we’re having this week to get through the questions. And I got a
bunch of questions, you guys, set in front of me. And, I’m gonna
start with that. And Julie, I don’t know that we can get to anymore
questions but can people still send in questions if they want to?
Julie McManus: 	Sure, I can monitor. If anybody has any last thoughts, they can
send them to feedback@makepeacetotalpackage.com and I can
shoot them over to you as I always do.
Bob Bly: 	Sure, and let’s give that e-mail again. Oh, you just gave it.
Julie McManus: 	Yeah, feedback@makepeacetotalpackage.com.
Bob Bly: 	Great. And, so let’s just dive into it. The first question is from
Hans and he wants to know, would you talk more about the
standard rate in data service you mentioned and how it should be
used to choose and contact potential clients?
Clayton Makepeace: 	Sure, standard rate and data service publishes a series of guides to
the various media and they have, there’s an S.R.D.S. for television
and radio and internet and all of this, you know, all the various
media. And, the direct mail S.R.D.S. is essentially a four inch
thick book that has somewhere in the neighborhood of, what is it
Bob, sixty thousand mailing lists that are available for rental. And,
each one of those, well, many of the lists, some of the lists are
compile lists which means that someone just went to a database
someplace and compiled names of companies or individuals. Like,
for example, the Dunn and Bradstreet lists are compiled lists of
companies and you can get any size company you want, any
number of employees, any amount of sales. Those are compile
lists.
There are also compile lists of doctors and lawyers and people by
profession. Those tend to be their addresses at work. The most
important list for us are the lists of direct response companies who
make their customer files available for rental. And, you’ll be able
to look up those companies by the type of businesses they’re in. If
you want to look at newsletter, who rent their files, you just simply
go to that section and you’ll see a listing of all of the newsletters
that are out there along with the size of their files.
WARNING:
The following transcript is raw, uncut, and may contain grammatical errors.

www.escriptionist.com 	Page 2 of 32
They’ll have, quite often, the number of hotline names, which is
the number of names, new customers they’ve brought in in the last
ninety days. That’ll tell ya how fast they’re growing. That’ll tell
you the mix, male to female. S.R.D.S. is a really excellent source
if you’re writing copy too because you can look at this and get
some really good demographic and psychographic data on the
people you’d be talking to, if your client is mailing to a given list.
Are you there Bob?
Bob Bly: 	Yes.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Okay. And so, you can look at each one of those listings and see
how large and successful the various companies are. It could be a
catalog company, a newsletter health supplement company, any
kind of company you want to write for, they’ll have listings in
there if they rent their list and most companies do. So, it’s just a
great place to go to get names of companies and also to research
when you write and copy. _____
Bob Bly: 	But Hans, I do, go ahead --
Clayton Makepeace: 	They’re fairly expense to buy but most libraries have them and,
you know, they have issues of the book going back years quite
often at the larger libraries, so.
Bob Bly: 	Or if your, this is what I did, if your library doesn’t have it, you
find a friend or a friend of a friend who’s in an ad agency and they
subscribe to it and they get a new directory every, like, three
months and they throw away the old one, it has no value to them,
they’ll give it to ya and ya know –
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yeah.
Bob Bly: 	-- it’ll save ya four or five hundred dollars. But, let me answer
Hans’s other question. We’re not trying to hound sale you
anything additional here but I, your question also says, I have it in
front of me, how to use S.R.D.S. but what other tool can you use to
select clients and how do you contact those clients? I have written
a book about this. ____ you don’t mind me mentioning it right?
Clayton Makepeace: 	No, go ahead.
Bob Bly: 	It’s called Secrets of a Freelance Writer and it’s a twelve dollar
paperback and tell ya everything you want to know about selling
yourself as a freelance copywriter, that we may not have covered

The Profit Center 	7_Q_and_A 	Page 3 of 32
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 3 of 32
here or that we’ve covered, a lot of what we covered here is not in
my book but it’ll tell ya that kind of, where you can find lists and
directories and what kind of, how you can contact them and it’s
available for, you know, eleven or twelve bucks on Amazon.com.
So, you might want to check that out at Standard Rate and Data
Service, if you want to check them out online. I believe it’s
S.R.D.S.com, you can check there.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Right. And, I think we mentioned it once before too but another
great resource is Target Marketing Online and you can just Google
Target Marketing you’ll, and find their website. They have a
directory of major mailers. It’s about an inch and a half, two
inches thick and it comes with, and a companion CD-Rom, just
pop into your computer and you can search for the kinds of
companies you’re looking for.
Bob Bly: 	Yeah, and that actually, I think is a terrific source for freelancers
______ so you can take a look at that as well. And that is, it’s not
cheap but it’s not as expensive as Standard Rate and Data Service.
It’s meant to be affordable to people who want to market to the
direct marketing industry.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yep.
Bob Bly: 	Here’s a question. What about, what is, Clayton, what is the usual
fee structure for multi-step mailings, a mailing campaign with a
series of two to ten pieces to a niche target market? I think what
she means is, you know, let’s say, you know, you get hired by,
like, you mentioned you used to work for someone who made
water purifiers and let’s say they had a list of people in their
market and they wanted to send a series of six self-mailers or
letters or postcards, how do you charge for that?
Clayton Makepeace: 	I would charge a percentage of revenues on something like that
because you’ll have, the largest mailings in a series like that would
tend to be a lead production, the lead production phase and, you
know, that’s a hard cost to the mailer and so he’s not gonna want
to add to his cost on that step by giving you a royalty. And, I think
if you’re doing the entire series, the simplest way to structure it
would be to say, “Okay, look, I’ll do the series for you but I want
five or ten percent of revenues.” And, you can define that any way
you want to. It would normally be net revenues, the total revenues
minus the marketing and fulfillment costs.
Bob Bly: 	Also, minus the product cost right?

-- 3 of 32 --

The Profit Center 	7_Q_and_A 	Page 4 of 32
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 4 of 32
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yeah, that would be the fulfillment cost.
Bob Bly: 	Right.
Clayton Makepeace: 	And, but I would structure it on a percentage of what I produced.
Alternately, you could do it on a percentage of the increase. So if,
right now, for example, his six step program is generating, you
know, and R.O.I. of, or let’s not do R.O.I., if his six step program
is generating fifty thousand a month, you come in and give him a
kick butt program and you generate a hundred thousand a month,
you would take a larger percentage of the increase. If you were
working just on a percentage of net revenues it would be, like, ten
percent say, just to pick a number. If you’re going in just to, if you
go in and take a percentage of the increase, his risk is reduced
substantially –
Bob Bly: 	Right.
Clayton Makepeace: 	-- there’s no way he can lose on that deal and if he was doing, let’s
say a hundred thousand a month before and you come in for ten
percent of revenues and you don’t lift his response rates then he’s
out ten thousand bucks cause he’s gotta pay you the ten thousand,
you know, the ten percent of a hundred thousand. But, if you come
in and you say, “Well, I’ll just take a percent of the increase,” that
is an easier deal for you to sell because there’s no risk to him and
you can ask, in return for taking the risk yourself you can ask for a
higher percentage.
Bob Bly: 	Got it, that’s a good answer. And, it says here, that was asked by
no name. So, no name we hope you’re satisfied. Find a name,
that’s a good thing for your business card. Here’s an interesting
question from our friend D.D. He says, “Can Clayton say more
about his procedures for using junior copywriters? For example,
working arrangements, training, compensation, contractual
arrangements, exclusivity or non-compete clauses.” And, this is
being asked from the point of view of someone who, Clayton, like
you, is also a senior copywriter and may want to do this.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Oh good, good. Yeah, when I’m working with a beginning
copywriter, somebody that may have a A.W.A.I. spec assignments
but nothing else to show, I, well, I begin by trying to match up
writers to assignments. I have coaching clients help product
clients help newsletter clients and investment clients. And so, I
look at the background of the person. The investment stuff, for
example, tends to get very technical and if they don’t have a

-- 4 of 32 --

The Profit Center 	7_Q_and_A 	Page 5 of 32
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 5 of 32
background there it would be a mistake to pair them up with an
investment client.
On the health side, things are a little easier because a lot of what
we write about is not technical and the research kits we get are
quite extensive. So, if, most writers can, I could probably find a
match there. The easiest of all, of course, is the coaching product
because we’re talking to people, predominantly who are
opportunity seekers and entrepreneurs trying to find a way to make
money and we can all relate to that and there’s not a lot of
technical stuff there.
But, I review their material, try to match them. The ones that
impress me, I then try to match with a client. I look for a project
that’s been assigned or about to be assigned and I match the writer
with that project. Then, I’ll have a preliminary telephone call with
the writer, in which I basically lay out what the job will be.
Yesterday, for example, I retained a young lady named Lisa to help
me on a package for health directions.
And, we had a telephone call, lasted maybe fifteen, twenty
minutes. I told her I was send her Federal Express and that the
client would also be sending one and asked her to look at it over
the weekend and told her that we would be talking on Monday.
So, on Monday she’s gonna be, she and I will be talking and she
will receive a document from me that will be a very detailed layout
of what I want the copy to look like. It’ll say, the main headline
will be forty-eight point impact, regular, not bold. It can be either
red, blue or green, not black.
The deck copy will be fourteen point times new roman bold. You
will not allow Bill Gates to do your indentions or your bulleted
lists or your numbered lists, you will do those manually. And, I
mean, it gets right down to the detail what I want it to look like.
Body text will be twelve point times new roman, subheads will be
fourteen point impact and they, too, can be red, blue or green. The
package will contain all collateral components including the
everything they get page, the response device, front and back
covers, carrier envelope copy, if applicable, you know, just
basically, I had, it’s a standing document that’s, I send out to all
new writers and it’s important that they do things that way so I
don’t have to spend a lot of time making the copy look presentable
to the client.
And also, it allows me to look at the page count and know exactly
where we are in terms of how well this copy will fit in to the

-- 5 of 32 --

The Profit Center 	7_Q_and_A 	Page 6 of 32
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 6 of 32
format that we’re going for. I’ve had experiences where
copywriters’ll send me running text in fourteen point to disguise
the fact that it, they wrote way to short for the format we were
writing for –
Bob Bly: 	Fourteen point triple spaced?
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yeah, exactly.
Bob Bly: 	What, David wants to also know, what about, do you have a
written contract with these people and –
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yeah.
Bob Bly: 	-- what’s the compensation?
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yeah, yeah. Let me get there in just a second.
Bob Bly: 	Sure.
Clayton Makepeace: 	The next thing that will happen with this writer is that, I will
include them in a conference call with the client, introduce ‘em to
the client. We will discuss the positioning of the product, we’ll
discuss the major theme that we want to go with and, in this case,
it’s a health newsletter so the, once everybody’s generally on board
with the theme then we will have a conference call with the editor
of the newsletter, the doctor who edits this newsletter. We’ll, on
that call, pick his brain. We know what we’re gonna write about,
now we want his solutions and his take on each of the things we’re
going to write about –
Bob Bly: 	Right.
Clayton Makepeace: 	After that call, while it’s still fresh in our minds, the writer and I
will get on the phone and I will literally dictate an outline and I’ll
say, “Okay, here’s the first,” you know, I don’t even address the
headline deck copy. I just start with the opening running text and I
say, “Okay, here’s the first thing we’re gonna say, here’s the
second thing we’re gonna say, here’s the third point we’re gonna
cover, the fourth point,” and I take ‘em all the way through until
we get up to the point where we begin asking for the sell.
Bob Bly: 	Right.
Clayton Makepeace: 	And then I ask the writer to please send me an outline of
everything we just discussed. And, this is a good, kind of, reality

-- 6 of 32 --

The Profit Center 	7_Q_and_A 	Page 7 of 32
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 7 of 32
check to make sure that the writer is getting it. And then, I take
their outline, I make whatever changes I want to make to it and
then I send it back to them and they write from that outline. And
again, this isn’t copy.
It’s just, the first thing we want to say is, we want to talk about
how terrible it is to have heart disease and the second thing we
want to say is how terrible what the medical community does to
people with heart disease. And then we want to talk about the fact
that there’s a better way. And then we want to introduce the editor
and say, “Hi, my name is Dr. Joe Schwartz and here are my
qualifications.” And, we want a credibility element and that could
be a series of testimonials or could be a short story about a patient
whose life he saved.
And then we introduce his solution to that particular problem. And
then we say, “And I want to tell you more about this in your free
copy of blank,” and that’s our premium, our first premium we’re
gonna give away. And then next we’re gonna move onto the next
health problem that this doctor is going to address and we’re going
to do the same thing. We’re going to empathize, we’re gonna warn
them about the solutions that are currently available to them, we’re
gonna present a better solution, we’re gonna tease five or six more
solutions and say that they’re in the second premium they’re going
to get.
And, we’re going to keep going like that until we’ve covered all of
our premiums and then we’re going to introduce the product,
justify our price, relieve the risk, ask for the sale. And so, that,
with that kind of outline the writer now is equipped to go and they,
at that point, will also have the full research kit. As far as contracts
and compensation are concerned, on the first assignment with a
new writer I typically will pay between four thousand and five
thousand dollars because I’m essentially paying them for the
privilege of writing it myself. If, and I tell them. I say, “There’s
gonna be more red on this page than on a slaughter house floor.
You have to not let that discourage you, this first project.”
I have a three strikes and you’re out policy but their first project is
never the first strike because that’s the learning process between
the two of us. And so, frankly if it were fair they’d be paying me a
hundred thousand dollars to teach them what I’m going to teach
them on this project. Instead, I’m willing to pay between four and
five thousand dollars. At the end of the project, then, we do a - and
we draw up a contract. There’s no royalty for the first three or four
projects at all for a young writer, for the simple fact that the

-- 7 of 32 --

The Profit Center 	7_Q_and_A 	Page 8 of 32
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 8 of 32
amount of time that I have to put into this to create a winner just
doesn’t justify it.
And, plus, it’s like getting paid to go to Harvard, you know?
You’re getting paid good money to do an internship with a top
writer.
Bob Bly: 	So you don’t give golf scholarships?
Clayton Makepeace: 	No. No, but if a person’s in the area we will have a toga party.
But anyway, the – let’s see where I was go, oh yeah, and so we
drop a contract and the contract is good until cancel and so it’s not
on a job per job basis. When I feel that the writer’s progressed to
the point where they’re making me money, we’re getting some
controls together, I will move them up depending on what I hope
will be a very fair assessment of what they’re bringing to the table
and get them to the point where they’re making half the advance in
each package, which would be twelve thousand five hundred.
And, I pay when the copy’s approved with young writers, not half
and half because, well, you know, it’s, sometimes with a younger
writer the process takes much, much longer.
So, and then, once they hit the twelve five per package advance
and they’re still progressing then I begin introducing royalties into
the picture, up to fifty percent of the total royalties, so depending
on whether it’s a wide-market product where I’m getting a smaller
royalty, typically thirty dollars a thousand, or a narrower-market
product where I’m getting fifty dollars a thousand or more, they’ll
receive up to fifty percent of that to keep them interested. We
don’t want to kid ourselves here. The young writer’s objective in
this relationship is to learn everything they can, get some samples,
make a name and then never write for me again because they’re
making so damn much money on their own they wouldn’t even
consider writing for someone else.
So, the objective of this is like a mother Eagle, you know, raising
her offspring. The ultimate objective is to push them out of the
nest. And so, that’s essentially my process. Now, I am working
with senior writers who are very happy with twelve five and
between fifteen and twenty-five dollars a thousand on projects I
copy chief for them because they tend to have very high hit rates,
more of our packages become controls. So, even senior writers
who make four, five hundred thousand a year, up to six hundred
thousand a year, come to me and ask me to copy chief them.

-- 8 of 32 --

The Profit Center 	7_Q_and_A 	Page 9 of 32
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 9 of 32
And, I think it’s a good exercise for everybody involved. Even if a
writer’s getting controls and making good money on the outside
and is fairly advanced, just the experience of working with
someone else and learning a different approach strengthens you.
And so, I have four or five senior writers whose names you would
know who have approached me over the last few months and asked
me to copy chief them on projects. And, so when I do that it’s a
fifty fifty split.
Bob Bly: 	So, this is open to copywriters at a wide range of levels?
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yes. I mean, I have I think the best, the hottest writer of
promotions for financial packages right now, I’m going to be copy
chiefing in June of next year. I’m working with another who’s
very well known to everybody on this call that I’m copy chiefing
right now on a health package. So, yeah, it’s very helpful for
everyone.
Bob Bly: 	That wouldn’t be me would it?
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yeah. I wouldn’t _____ --
Bob Bly: 	I have no secrets.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Okay.
Bob Bly: 	I’m proud. I mean, people, to know how seriously we take this,
cause I think it’s important and people are saying, “Well, you
know, why would you do this?” You know, I don’t make the
money Clayton makes but I make a lot of money. I mean, I’ll
make over seven hundred thousand dollars this year. But, when
you work with any other writer who’s good you’re gonna learn a
lot. And, when you work with someone of Clayton’s caliber
you’re gonna learn even more. And, I think all of the very
successful copywriters who are operating today, correct me if you
think I’m wrong, have been copy chief or mentored at some time
or another by other people at or above their level.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yeah. I mean, Gary Bencivenga was mentored by Dan Rosenthal.
Bob Bly: 	There you go.
Clayton Makepeace: 	That’s what got him started. So, you know, after he left David
Ogilvy’s organization. So, yeah, I think it’s very helpful. And,
especially if you’ve been doing it for a while, it’s a great way to
kind of get a fresh take on everything and –

-- 9 of 32 --

The Profit Center 	7_Q_and_A 	Page 10 of 32
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 10 of 32
Bob Bly: 	It totally freshens you.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yeah.
Bob Bly: 	If you do it for a while and you have some level of success, you
start to repeat yourself –
Clayton Makepeace: 	Right.
Bob Bly: 	And to do it, you know, and you, theoretically you should study
other copywriter’s work but studying their work is not the same as
being mentored or copy chief by them. So –
Clayton Makepeace: 	Well, I think –
Bob Bly: 	Go ahead.
Clayton Makepeace: 	I think the key is the mental processes involved. You know, you
can study their copy but that, you know, and that’s good but you
don’t really know how they arrived at particular decisions or ideas
as they were developing that copy. And so, working with them
allows you to see those mental processes, makes ‘em transparent to
ya.
Bob Bly: 	Now, we have a question from Peter. It’s questioning, slightly
cynical but I’m gonna read it. If you are making the very, very big
bucks you are Clayton, and believe me folks he is. Why are you
sharing all your secrets for us, with us so cheap? Gary Bensivango
sold his for five thousand dollars. What’s the real reason behind
holding all these seminars?
Clayton Makepeace: 	I get that a lot.
Bob Bly: 	Wait, wait, I gotta right down my list. Kill Peter, no we’re only
joking Peter. Peter, we’re only joking.
Clayton Makepeace: 	No, I get that a lot. Actually it’s Carlene’s fault. She came to me
about a year and a half ago and because and, actually, we were at
the Willard in Washington, D.C., Wendy and I were and just
hanging out --
Bob Bly: 	Right.
Clayton Makepeace: 	-- and Carline came over and we had a nice day together and she
had just been to Gary’s conference. No, she was planning to go to

-- 10 of 32 --

The Profit Center 	7_Q_and_A 	Page 11 of 32
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 11 of 32
Gary’s conference, that was it. And, she said, you know, because
Gary has never really mentored writers and she said, “You know,
the guy who should be doing this kind of stuff is you,” because she
felt that I had the ability not to, to do more than, you know, just
share what I do but to actually teach others to do it.
And, so she kept nagging me and nagging me and nagging me until
I finally said all right already, I’ll do it. I could do it easy. But,
anyone who reads The Total Package knows that up until the
A.W.A.I. conference, I was not somebody who wanted to ever be
in front of an audience or do public speaking. And so, the idea of
doing a conference was not something that I really felt was
something I wanted to do. So, the _______ was a nice way for me
to be able to start connecting with other writers and sharing what I
knew.
I was also having the problem, and still am, with clients who want
me to do far more than I can do. And, although, you know, seven
figures is very nice, you know, there’s eight figures out there
available to me, if I only had the capacity to produce more copy.
And, I had learned when I was with Weiss that the copy _____ I
had worked with previously, Parris Lampropulous and, you know,
Bob Hutchinson and Kent Kome and Carlene and others, made me
money because when I was, you know, booked solid, which was a
hundred percent of the time, and another project came in I could
accept it because I had somebody that could work with me on it.
And so, I thought that I could do well by doing good here by
helping young writers come along. And, at the same time, out of
this group of thousands of young writers, find people who would
be, who I’d want to be in business with and who would increase
my capacity. And, that’s precisely what’s happened. I have seven
copy cubs. Right now all of them have come out of the files of
The Total Package. All of them have completed at least the first
A.W.A.I. course and, like I was telling the people at A.W.A.I., I
love that course, I recommend it to everyone.
However, it’s like going to college. You know, and I think I’ve
used this metaphor before but it’s like going to college and getting
your degree in medicine. They don’t allow you just to go out and
start cutting on people, you have to go serve an internship. And,
this is the same thing. I mean, we make more money than the best
paid cardiologists in the country to write copy, all right. They go
to college for twelve years and serve an internship and a residency
before they are allowed to practice their craft.

-- 11 of 32 --

The Profit Center 	7_Q_and_A 	Page 12 of 32
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 12 of 32
Bob Bly: 	Yeah, they really don’t start making money. Everybody says
doctors are so well paid. First of all, they don’t start making
money until they’re in their thirties.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yeah. Well, we’re in a business where you can take Masterson’s
courses –
Bob Bly: 	Right.
Clayton Makepeace: 	-- and then be in an internship with the top copywriter in a matter
of months with the potential to make far more than any doctor or
cardiologist in the country. And so, I mean, it’s a wonderful
pursuit, but to think that, you know, taking the course is going to
get you immediately, you know, the minute they say, “Okay,
you’re done,” immediately, you know, into the ranks of mid to
high six figure writers is just a little bit over optimistic. And, the
internship is a transition that I can provide for people and it makes
me money and it makes them money and my clients benefit
because, you know, as I’ve said so many times, the scarce resource
in the direct response industry is not product ideas, it’s not editors
of newsletter, you know, their newsletters, they’re growing on
trees. You can throw a rock and hit somebody that wants to edit
their own newsletter. The scarce resource is the copywriter that
can sell it to prospects. So, it’s just good for everybody.
Bob Bly: 	But I think –
Clayton Makepeace: 	So that’s how I got into it.
Bob Bly: 	Yeah, and I think the, really apt, for people listening, especially
A.W.A.I. recent graduates, is this idea of a residency. You know,
you don’t go from medical school, you know, doctors train and this
is training. Now, terrific. Now, we have a question here from
Mark. He says, “Aside from you, Clayton, do you know of other
top copywriters who take on copy cubs?
Clayton Makepeace: 	Parris Lampropulous does.
Bob Bly: 	Okay, anybody else?
Clayton Makepeace: 	Carline Cole.
Bob Bly: 	Harris, Carlene Cole.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yeah and –

-- 12 of 32 --

The Profit Center 	7_Q_and_A 	Page 13 of 32
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 13 of 32
Bob Bly: 	Steve Wexler does.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Okay.
Bob Bly: 	And he’s got ads in the back of D.M. News every issue.
Clayton Makepeace: 	I’m not aware of others who do but I think – I’m trying to
remember. Somebody was telling me the other day that someone
else is taking on cubs.
Bob Bly: 	I don’t know who that would be.
Clayton Makepeace: 	I don’t want to say the wrong name.
Bob Bly: 	Yeah.
Clayton Makepeace: 	But, the answer is yes, other people are. And, as word gets out, I
mean, we’re in a situation where every time I talk to a client now –
I have one client who has us on a quarter a million dollar a year
retainer and we’ve only been on the retainer for thirty days and he
doubled it yesterday because I, you know, I had come along with a
couple more writers that I could throw at his project and we were
on the phone. He was complaining that he wasn’t getting enough
output from us. And so I said, “Well, you know, we can do the
work now because I have these two new web writers that we’ve
been mentoring for the last two months and they’re really good and
would just bring ‘em in under Danny Levis,” who’s my main web
guy for this particular client, my only web guy for this client.
And so, I’m gonna slip them in under Danny and he’ll be able to
produce two to three times more work for this client. But just, and,
literally in a short conversation yesterday, the retainer went from a
quarter million to a half million a year. So, as most, you know, as
other writers see what we’re doing, I expect this practice to
become more commonplace throughout the community.
Bob Bly: 	Got it. Here’s a question. I thought we did this one but I guess we
didn’t. Clayton, how do you know beforehand when a concept
will be a winner? If you’ve come up with a number of headlines or
concepts, for example, how do you decide which ones of those are
worth testing?
Clayton Makepeace: 	For me it’s visceral. It’s just, it’s –
Bob Bly: 	I thought you’d say that.

-- 13 of 32 --

The Profit Center 	7_Q_and_A 	Page 14 of 32
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 14 of 32
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yeah, it’s not, I’m not a formula writer. I think rules are for
unimaginative slugs. I hate rules. I’m a libertarian by nature and
if I don’t feel it in my guy it sucks. If I do feel it in my gut it’s
good. And, the strong I feel it the stronger it is. So, you know, I
will come, every once in a while, to a situation where I’ll say,
“You know, I have three concepts, all of which grab me but I don’t
know which one is gonna resonate with my market best.” And, in
that case, I beg and plead with the client to do a three panel test on
the first trial of the copy.
It’s not always possible but they will usually do two if you ask real
nice. But, again, it’s a visceral thing, it comes down to feeling. If
you read that headline and you feel like you’re at the site of a train
wreck that’s about to happen, you cannot look away, you know,
then you have a winner. If you’re rationalizing, intellectualizing it,
over thinking it, and you end up with something that it may be
benefit laden but flat, you know, you have all of the rational
reasons why it’s a good headline cause you read all the books and
it follows all the rules, but if it’s not grabbing you by the short and
curlies, it’s not a good headline, you know, so.
Bob Bly: 	I think, yeah, I think that’s how most would ultimately answer.
It’s visceral, you either have an instinct for what sales or you
develop it over time.
Clayton Makepeace: 	You know, that’s, I was thinking about this morning. I’m glad you
said that cause, the bottom line is that we are salespeople, that’s all
we are. We do it with ink or with bits and bites on the internet but
we’re salespeople and that’s it. I mean, the number one skill that
you can acquire as a copywriter is to become a good salesman. If
you can do that then it doesn’t matter how great a linguist you are,
you know, you just talk to your prospects as a good salesman and
you close them. You don’t have to have wonderful pros. Your
stuff doesn’t have to even be grammatically correct.
But, if you can motivate them to make a purchase then you’re
gonna be a great copywriter and, frankly, the best copywriters are
not the best writers. The best copywriters are the best salespeople.
And, when you talk to copywriters who have succeeded and who
have done really well in their careers, many of them have one thing
in common. At some point in their life they were a door to door
salesman, they sold used cars, they sold insurance, they sold
something, you know, face to face and they developed a feel for
the prospect. So, you know, sharpening your sales skills, to me, is
far more important than simply sharpening your writing skills.

-- 14 of 32 --

The Profit Center 	7_Q_and_A 	Page 15 of 32
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 15 of 32
Bob Bly: 	Good point and I would wholly disagree. Here’s a question, just
switching, totally switching gears. 	Do you ever write, copy
directly in design software, I get asked this all the time, such as
cork or end design? It would seem like this would be especially
useful for graphic intensive pieces such as magalogs and tabloids
to get a better idea of how it will eventually look when it’s laid out
and to keep you from over or under writing.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yes but, I went through a phase where I wrote everything in cork
and cork is a pretty good program to do that in because it’s so
simple to use, you don’t have –
Bob Bly: 	Right.
Clayton Makepeace: 	-- it’s not all the fussing around with text boxes and stuff and
linking that you have to do within design or page maker. The, I
found myself paying too much attention to the design too early in
the process and it was distracting to me. So, I stopped doing it that
way. I know other writers who continue to write using design
software. One of them in particular is an excellent writer, others,
you know, I’m not that impressed with their output.
My feeling is that, first of all, writing to fit is a mistake. Don’t
ever even think about the length of the piece while you’re writing.
That’s just, that’s a distraction, it’s not something you have to
concern yourself about. When you’re writing, the most important
thing you can do is to get every benefit, every reason of why and
every resonant emotion out on paper. If that think runs a hundred
and twenty pages, God bless ya, you know, it’s always easier to cut
then it is to add.
Bob Bly: 	Right. But, conversely, you can use the same approach. Write it
to what you think works and then, you know, if it’s too short and
insubstantial, there’s probably more in the research you have that
can go back to beef up points where you’re weak.
Clayton Makepeace: 	To me, that’s getting the cart before the horse. And, again, this is
subjective. You know, the answer to the question really is, do
whatever works for you. But, in my experience, you know, what’s
worked for me is, focus on the prospect, connect the prospect to
the product, milk every single benefit, dimensionalize every single
benefit, connect every benefit with an emotion that the prospect
has, be obsessed with the sale.
Then, when you’re through, look down and say, “Oh, this is thirty-
four pages long, I’m close,” you know? You know, I may have to

-- 15 of 32 --

The Profit Center 	7_Q_and_A 	Page 16 of 32
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 16 of 32
cut a page but I’m pretty good. Or, look at it and say, “Holy cow,
it’s sixty-four pages long, I’m gonna have to really do some
serious cutting here.” But what you’re cutting from is a document
that has every single sales point that you could possibly come up
with –
Bob Bly: 	Right.
Clayton Makepeace: 	And so, in, you’re not, you don’t necessarily have to cut sales
points, you just condense and you prioritize. When you write to fit
it’s an artificial limitation, you know, it says the length of this
thing is more important than its selling power.
Bob Bly: 	And, people learn that. It’s so odd. Do you remember when you
were in sixth grade and the teacher said, “I need a paper on this or
an essay on this three pages,” that was the only thing that was
important to her, which was a very –
Clayton Makepeace: 	Right.
Bob Bly: 	-- bad way to teach you.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yeah, and then you sat there writing, “It was a very, very, very,
very, very cold day,” you know?
Bob Bly: 	Great Expectations is –
Clayton Makepeace: 	______ could –
Bob Bly: 	-- an interesting book. It was very interesting to read it and I was
interested
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yeah. But, again, the length as an artificial, absolutely artificial
criteria.
Bob Bly: 	I’m glad to hear ya say that.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yeah.
Bob Bly: 	Here’s an interesting question from Uin. He says, “What do you
do when your research” – now he gives this for a financial
newsletter but this could work with any product. “What do you do
when your research digs up very compelling evidence against your
client’s editorials in the financial newsletter business?” This guy,
he says, “So, what if, you know, you’re writing for an investment

-- 16 of 32 --

The Profit Center 	7_Q_and_A 	Page 17 of 32
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 17 of 32
newsletter and your research finds that the guy is wrong, what he
says?”
Clayton Makepeace: 	Well, you’ve got two choices really. If you’re gonna be a
successful copywriter you need to put your own opinions aside and
realize they don’t matter. You’re not the financial expert, you’re
not the person with the product, your client is. You don’t have his
qualifications to make these determinations. Your job, he has an
opinion and your job is to sell that opinion. If you can’t do that,
whether out of good conscience or because you feel negative about
his opinions, then you should resign the account and give him his
money back.
But, a professional copywriter is just that, he’s a professional. He
recognizes that what he’s selling, that when he accepts an
assignment, his job, he becomes an actor. Okay? To me, a
copywriter who says, “I don’t agree with my client’s point of view
therefore, you know, I can’t writ his copy,” is like an actor saying,
“I can’t play Jack the Ripper because I would never kill anybody.”
You know?
Bob Bly: 	Right. There was a funny radio commercial on where the actor is
asked to read some lines in the radio commercial and he says,
“Well, what’s my motivation,” and the producer says, “We’re
paying you.”
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yeah. That’s exactly right. You’re a professional and you’re in an
actor’s role. You’re putting yourself in the shoes of somebody
who does hold this opinion. And, you have to discipline your –
now, look, there are times when you look at something someone’s
saying and you say, “This guy’s so obviously wrong, my
conscience prohibits me from being involved with this product or
presenting this opinion.”
Let’s say, for example, you are a devout republican and the guy is
a democrat and he’s voicing moral positions or political positions
that you have find abhorrent. If that’s the case, and you have a
moral aversion to what he’s saying, then don’t accept it in the first
place or resign the account. If you find it out later, resign the
account and give him his money back. Short of that, recognize that
just like an attorney is an advocate for his client, you’re an
advocate for your client and your job is not to have an opinion,
your job is to sell the editor’s opinion or the opinion of the figure
head or the advocate for the product.

-- 17 of 32 --

The Profit Center 	7_Q_and_A 	Page 18 of 32
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 18 of 32
Bob Bly: 	Got it. And, I would agree. It’s interesting. You know who Jerry
Hunsinger is?
Clayton Makepeace: 	Sure.
Bob Bly: 	Yeah, the, he’s one of the top fundraising ____ of all time and in a
conference call I did with him he told, someone asked this and he
said, “Well, you know,” the same thing you did about being the
clients advocate and he says he’d never had as much fun as when
he was writing for two clients at the same time on, one was on the
left side of the issue, whatever it was, pro-choice or anti-choice,
whatever it was, and the other was on, you know, the other was on
the flip side and –
Clayton Makepeace: 	Right.
Bob Bly: 	-- you know, and again, you’re next the expert, you want to
advocate or interpret the client’s point of view. Now, here’s an
interesting question. Let’s say you are a new, we don’t have to say
young, we often use the term young, but you’re just new to
freelance copywriting. So, you’re, you know, you’re what you
would call a young writer, even though you may be older, and you
want to start off and a client approaches you and say, “Hey, I need
a, you know, I need a direct mail package to sell a health product
or a newsletter subscription.”
Maybe, let’s say it’s a letter package and it’s gonna be a, you
know, an eight, twelve, or sixteen page letter. If I’m new and I’m
lucky enough to get asked to do that, what combination of upfront
fee and royalty should I charge given my newbie status?
Clayton Makepeace: 	I guess, are we talking about our industry, are we talking about
newsletter industry here?
Bob Bly: 	We can talk about, he doesn’t specify industry but let’s talk about,
for example, let’s just talk about, you’re asked to do a newsletter
package. Maybe you’re not asked by Phillips or Gore but you’re
asked by one of the second, you know, the smaller newsletter
publishers, they have a couple of newsletters that, you know, let’s
say it’s a mutual fund newsletter, they come to you, they, you
know, they, you know, and what should you charge? Now, I’m
gonna, before you answer, I think a good way to sort of judge this
in advance is to ask what copywriters they’re using now because if
they, or the names are Clayton _______ or Peter Buetel or they’re
using A-list copywriters, you know something about that client’s

-- 18 of 32 --

The Profit Center 	7_Q_and_A 	Page 19 of 32
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 19 of 32
ability to pay a decent fee. 	I’m not saying you should charge the
same fee but that tells you something.
Clayton Makepeace: 	That’s right. But, you know, if they’re coming to you and you’re a
beginning writer and you have no track record –
Bob Bly: 	That’s what the question says.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yeah, they, I mean, let’s face it, most of the clients – the answer is
kind of a two part answer I think. There are two kinds of clients
out there. There are clients out there who are used to working with
freelance copywriters and if they are they’re, they have a certain
number in their head when they talk to writer. They know that
they’re gonna have to pay, for example, a twenty-five thousand
dollar advance and a royalty. If someone comes to them, though,
and they really like your copy, even though it may not be for their
industry and you may have no track record, they’re taking a huge
risk by hiring you. And, if they decide to hire you anyway, you
know, they must really believe in you.
But remember, you know, when you write the copy, it has to be
designed, that’s gonna cost between, if it’s direct mail, between six
and twelve thousand dollars to do the graphic design for the
package. Then they’ve got print prep which can be ten, twenty
thousand dollars. Then they’ve got to print up the pieces
themselves and if they do, say, fifty thousand name test that’s
gonna cost ‘em probably another, you know, thirty, forty thousand
dollars to mail it.
Bob Bly: 	Right.
Clayton Makepeace: 	So, you know, by the time they’re through, they’re into this, not
even counting your fee, fifty, sixty thousand dollars that they’re
betting that you can write them a winner. So, that’s why they’re
willing to pay the high fee to writers with known track records.
Bob Bly: 	They want you to put time into it and take it seriously and make it
worth your while you’re saying?
Clayton Makepeace: 	Well, I’m saying that track record is everything with these guys.
They’re willing to pay the big fees because I can give them four or
five times the probability of getting a control and four or five times
of probability of not losing money than a writer with a –
Bob Bly: 	A beginner.

-- 19 of 32 --

The Profit Center 	7_Q_and_A 	Page 20 of 32
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 20 of 32
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yeah, without a, with less of a track record and especially with no
track record.
Bob Bly: 	Right.
Clayton Makepeace: 	So, short answer, I would think, if they’re willing to pay you five
to six thousand dollars would be a good place to start on the
advance side.
Bob Bly: 	Right, and how about the –
Clayton Makepeace: 	Okay.
Bob Bly: 	-- royalty side.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Full blown.
Bob Bly: 	So, full blown royalty but a lower advance, five or six –
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yeah.
Bob Bly: 	-- grand? That’s in line with what I’ve heard other people quote so
I think that’s a very good answer to that.
Clayton Makepeace: 	I have to tell you that, don’t ever accept the argument that you
should accept a lower royalty. I think we covered that earlier –
Bob Bly: 	Yes we did.
Clayton Makepeace: 	-- in the series.
Bob Bly: 	And the people can go back and listen to that.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yeah.
Bob Bly: 	But, you explained that in detail and very eloquently.
Julie McManus: 	Too, just to interject –
Bob Bly: 	Yeah.
Julie McManus: 	-- from a time perspective, cause I don’t know how long we’re
planning to go today.
Bob Bly: 	Clayton what do you want to do? What did we say, 1:00 p.m., you
want to go a litter longer?

Clayton Makepeace: 	Oh sure.
Bob Bly: 	Okay.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yeah.
Bob Bly: 	So, Julie, we’ll do that then.
Julie McManus: 	Okay.
Bob Bly: 	Does that work for everyone?
Julie McManus: 	Yep.
Bob Bly: 	Okay. So, let me get, next one, let me click on my e-mail reader
cause questions are coming in even as we speak, see what those are
and there’s a couple. So, we answered what we should charge,
five or six thousand dollars up front and the same royalty that an
experienced writer would get. Joan asked, “What’s the best source
of finding upcoming marketing conferences to network with
potential clients,” and then within that is the question, “should
people go to conferences and network or is that a waste of time in
your opinion?”
Clayton Makepeace: 	Conferences are an excellent opportunity for writers. And there
are, you know, there’s any number of D.M.A. conferences around
the country. The next, I think, national one is San Francisco next
fall. When is D.M. Days in New York Bob?
Bob Bly: 	I don’t know when it is but if you go on the, just Google Direct
Marketing Association. You can go on their website and find all
their conferences. I mean, and they’re other organizations but
that’s the biggie.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yeah, that’s the big one. Check them out and they’ll give you a
full listing of all of their conferences. Also check out N.E.P.A.
that’s the old newsletter on newsletters. It’s the newsletter
association. If you’re interested in writing for newsletters,
N.E.P.A. has conferences as well throughout the year.

-- 21 of 32 --

The Profit Center 	7_Q_and_A 	Page 22 of 32
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 22 of 32
Bob Bly: 	Right. And they’re at www.newsletters.org, that’s an easy one to
remember.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yeah. And also, we’re going to be doing our first ever, and who
knows, maybe our last one, live in event in April of 2006. We’re
looking at the 20th, 21st and 22 nd and we’ll be announcing – we’re
going to be actually asking you to help us design the conference
and in a special e-mail that you’ll be receiving in the next couple
weeks and asking your advice, asking you to tell us what it is you
would like us to show you at that conference. And, so kind of
pencil us in there because I would really love to have you all there.
I think it’s gonna be a great time –
Bob Bly: 	And so mark that date. Tentatively, April 26, 27, around –
Clayton Makepeace: 	No, no, no, 20, 21, 22.
Bob Bly: 	20, 21, 22.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yeah, and we’ll be sending a questionnaire in a couple of weeks
and asking you to help us with it and then a week or so after that
we’ll be tabulating the results and announcing the event so.
Bob Bly: 	Julie, you want to take my credit card now? I want to go. Wait a
minute. I don’t want to give my credit card number over this
phone line. Everybody else is sitting there with a pencil.
Julie McManus: 	I would mute it so.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yeah, we’d all be going on –
Bob Bly: 	Yeah.
Clayton Makepeace: 	-- Bob’s dime.
Bob Bly: 	All right, I’ll do that privately.
Julie McManus: 	You want to give me that number Bob?
Bob Bly: 	No, I got smart.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Let me say a couple more things about conferences. The big
D.M.A. conferences are good but they’re huge and you’re gonna
be a small fish in a huge pond and everybody is trying to sell
somebody something at those conferences. The good news is,
they’re not prohibitively expense and they’re enough of them

-- 22 of 32 --

The Profit Center 	7_Q_and_A 	Page 23 of 32
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 23 of 32
during the year that are local conferences, there’s a Direct
Marketing Days in New York, there’s a D.M. Days L.A., they’re
all over the country –
Bob Bly: 	Washington has a big active chapter, the D.M.A.W.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yeah. And, so they’re not prohibitively expensive, that’d be fairly
close and you’ll be able to meet some people. But just now, when
you go to that you’re gonna, every printer in the area’s gonna be
there, every list broker’s gonna be there and they’re all gonna be
chasing anything that sniffs like a client and you’re going to feel
like the, you know, twelve year old girl in a lobby full of rock
stars –
Bob Bly: 	I would have said chum in a shark pool but.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yeah. And so the, to me the better opportunity are conferences
like the A.W.A.I. conference where you go and there’re serious
copywriters there and there are clients who also go to those. And,
the A.W.A.I. conference is still relatively new. I was very
impressed this time at the job fair to see Motley Fool there _____
was there and they’re looking for copywriters. So, I think those
kinds of conferences would be excellent. I think my conference
would be excellent too cause we’ll have a good mix of major
clients and copywriters there.
And when you’re there, when you’re at a conference like this, I
think, you know, there are two ways to go with it. First of all, go
prepared, go loaded for bear, have your sample, have a pitch letter
for the people that are gonna be there who hire writers. It
demonstrates the quality of your writing as well as, you know,
introduces yourself, and, go there hoping to make a contact. I, in
the __________ interview, he talked about how he went to a Garry
Albert conference and came away with a, three assignments from
that one conference and he still has that one client now, to date, a
decade later and that client pays him seven figures a year.
So, but that wasn’t a big D.M.A. conference, that was a copywriter
conference. The other thing that I would urge you to do when you
go to conferences, and this can only happen easily at a copywriter
conference, by that I mean, you know, whether it’s a large
percentage of people there or other copywriters, we talked today
about me mentoring other writers, even major writers like Bob.
Every one of you is a copywriter or an aspiring copywriter and you
guys need to be talking to each other. You need to be in, you need

-- 23 of 32 --

The Profit Center 	7_Q_and_A 	Page 24 of 32
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 24 of 32
to be creating cells of copywriters, friends that you meet at these
conferences.
Go out, have a cocktail with ‘em. Sit down, work out an
arrangement where you can share your copy with each other and
____ each other’s copy and sharpen each other that way. I think
that’s an –
Bob Bly: 	That’s a great suggestion.
Clayton Makepeace: 	-- you know, an outstanding opportunity that’s just being missed,
you know. And, so, you know, when you go to these conferences,
be sociable. These are not your competitors, they’re your friends.
They’re in the same boat you are. They can sharpen you and you
can sharpen them. And then twenty years from now when you’re
all sitting around on your front porches talking about how much
money you made, you know, you’ll have friends to talk about, you
know, how you guys got started together, so.
Bob Bly: 	I agree with that terrific suggestion. Here’s a question, this comes
up a lot from people who get into copywriting. You mentioned
how it’s good to have a sales background in copywriting, but
copywriters, as you know, come from varied backgrounds. Some
come from just the corporate work. Some come from sales
backgrounds but a lot of ‘em come from writing backgrounds.
They were newspaper reporters or they were journalists and they
decided to do another type of writing. And, these guys always ask
me, here’s a particular question from Michael but I’ve been asked
this many times. People will say, “I’m a writer. Where can I learn
about marketing, cause I don’t know marketing. Where can I learn
modern marketing strategies that can make both the client and me
more successful?”
Clayton Makepeace: 	There’re a couple of sources of that. First of all, get Bob Stone’s
book, Successful Direct Marketing Methods. It’s a wonderful
book on how direct marketing works. Bob also has written several
books that explain the nuts and bolts and the rationale behind
direct marketing.
Bob Bly: 	You mean me or Bob Stone?
Clayton Makepeace: 	You.
Bob Bly: 	Yes, and you can get those – I’ll just mention. If you go to my
website, www.bly.com, there’s a lot of content, articles on there on

-- 24 of 32 --

The Profit Center 	7_Q_and_A 	Page 25 of 32
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 25 of 32
the subject of marketing. You click on articles and those are free,
you don’t have to pay for them. And then, if you want to see the
books I’ve written, you go to bly.com and click on books and you
can see and you can order them directly from the site.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Now, I hesitate to mention this but there are a couple of guys on
the internet, both who claim to be the best marketing experts in the
history of the universe –
Bob Bly: 	I gotta hear this. Go ahead, mention it. Let’s have fun.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Okay. And, they have enormous egos and when they sell
something to you you’re going to pay, believe me, top dollar for it.
But, they have free ______ and those ______, quite often, contain
a lot of great ideas that you could take to your clients to strengthen
their campaigns. So, I think they’re probably cheaper sources of
the same information –
Bob Bly: 	Although the _____ are free you’re saying?
Clayton Makepeace: 	-- that they’re, that they, yeah. They’re cheaper sources of the
information that they sell.
Bob Bly: 	Right.
Clayton Makepeace: 	But, what they give away in their ______ is very valuable.
Bob Bly: 	So what are these _____?
Clayton Makepeace: 	Okay, go to Abraham.com and sign up for Jay’s free _____ and
Google Dan Kennedy. Dan is a copywriter but in – and I’ve only
heard him speak once and he’s great. He’s great on the stump and
he’s hilarious and I totally agree with everything that I’ve heard
him say. But, Dan goes beyond just copy and, in fact, most of the
things that I’ve heard him talk about had more to do with
marketing. So, those are two more free sources that you can go to.
But, read Successful Direct Marketing Methods. That’s the book
that opened my eyes when I first got into this business and showed
me exactly how the numbers work and what the limitations and
opportunities are, how responses counted and then all of those
boring numbers that I had in my arithmetic issue last week. And, it
just gives you a good founding and, as I pointed out before, most
of the money I’ve made in this money, the really big pay days
came from not just writing the copy but conceiving the campaigns

-- 25 of 32 --

The Profit Center 	7_Q_and_A 	Page 26 of 32
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 26 of 32
and designing the marketing strategies behind them. So, getting a
grasp on that stuff is an excellent idea.
Bob Bly: 	Right. And I’d also, I mean, there’s a set of books that are the
standards of the industry and people quote them and you guys have
heard them before, the books by David Ogelby, especially Ogelby
on advertising, Testing Advertising Methods by John Caples but
Bob Stone is certainly at the top of that list and –
Clayton Makepeace: 	Well, Stone is a marketing guy more than a copywriter –
Bob Bly: 	Right.
Clayton Makepeace: 	-- and, a lot of the other books I’ve seen out there more, you know,
like Ogilvy and Caples and Reeves, those are books on writing ads,
you know. But Stone’s book is really good on the nuts and bolts,
so.
Bob Bly: 	And supposedly, I’ve never read it but, what’s his name? Ed
Burnett also has a compendium, I think it’s Direct Mail
Encyclopedia that’s also of a similar nature that you might take a
look at, and he’s a good guy.
Clayton Makepeace: 	I just, just one other –
Bob Bly: 	Yeah.
Clayton Makepeace: 	-- little thing. If you were an English teacher or if you were a
journalist and you decided that you want to be a copywriter, one of
the best things, and this is my advice, Bob, you may disagree but,
my advice is, get a job at night on a car lot or selling insurance or
doing something in the sales side of things. You don’t have to
make it a career, it doesn’t have to be full-time but at least have the
experience of approaching a prospect and earning your yes and
overcoming objections and doing the things that are necessary to
make a sell.
Bob Bly: 	And, if you really don’t want to do this in person and you’re
bashful, I think you can get a lot of the same experience, I did this,
getting a job, I didn’t do it for a long time, telemarketing. Not
from a script but telephone selling. I sold a high-end product
where it was, I wasn’t reading a script, where I was given
guidelines and you get what people really think and how they’ll
talk to you.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yep.

-- 26 of 32 --

The Profit Center 	7_Q_and_A 	Page 27 of 32
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 27 of 32
Bob Bly: 	Here’s a question from someone who, John, who wants to break
into writing for the newsletter industry, which you have a lot of
experience in. He says, “I have the Oxbridge Directory of
Newsletters. How do I select which newsletter publishers to
approach about doing copywriting?”
Clayton Makepeace: 	Good question. I guess it would, a degree, it depends on your
experience level. Let’s assume that you’re a beginner in the field.
I would be – the majors are not going to want to higher somebody
without a track record. The majors being Phillips, ______,
Bordram, Rodale, which isn’t newsletters but one shot books and –
Bob Bly: 	Right.
Clayton Makepeace: 	-- and subscriptions. Who’d I leave out? Healthy Directions,
they’re not gonna want to higher out of the box, somebody that
isn’t experienced yet or doesn’t have really great samples to show.
Now, if you have tremendous spec assignments for A.W.A.I., there
is nothing wrong, at all, with sending ‘em to Ed Elliot at Healthy
Directions, which some of you have done. Let me tell you what
happens next.
Ed sends ‘em to me. Or, he’ll just call you up and say, “Hey,
thanks for the sample but, you know, we’re hiring through
Clayton.” So, I think with these larger publishers, the ones I’ve
named, and Lombardi would be one of those too, probably, you
may have a little trouble getting in the front door with no
experience. That isn’t to say you shouldn’t approach them though,
and I certainly would and show your samples to them.
But, there are thousands of small one thousand, two thousand, five
thousand subscriber newsletters out there that you’ll find in
Oxbridge. And, those are the ones that, I would make sure that I
have a healthy helping those on any prospect list that I develop.
I’m assuming you’re using the formula that I laid out where you
contact these people by a series of mailings. I just had this done to
me, by the way, and it was wonderful. Last Wednesday, in my
new offices here, there was a six by nine orange postcard. And,
the postcard said, “In two days, the most important package you
have received all year will arrive in your mailbox.”
And I, the guys from Agora were here visiting and I brought it up
and I was waiving it over my head and I said, “Look, one of the
total package people, you know, has taken my advice,” and Lisa, if
you’re on the call hi. “And, I just can’t wait to see what comes

-- 27 of 32 --

The Profit Center 	7_Q_and_A 	Page 28 of 32
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 28 of 32
next.” And, sure enough, two days later I get a D.H.L. package
and it’s got this wonderful thing in there about how Harleys and
marketing go together and she’s a biker from Connecticut and it
was just really good example of great writing.
And, but this still wasn’t the big package. The big package was
not going to arrive for another couple of days. So, she had me
sitting on pins and needles to see what she was gonna do next.
And so, I basically had my own thing pulled on me, you know, my
own strategy pulled on me and I loved every minute of it. I called
her yesterday and hired her and I was kind of laughing about it
because it took grit, you know, it took courage and by golly, she
pulled it off, it worked. It was really well executed.
I think the real opportunity here for beginning writers is with these
smaller newsletters that are published by their editors rather than
by big publishing companies. Do one or two of those, produce
some degree of success with those then you can walk into a Gore
or a Phillips or Healthy Directions or any of these other larger
companies and you can do it in as little as, you know, sixty, ninety,
a hundred and twenty days with an actual sample under your arm.
So that’s –
Julie McManus: 	One other thing too, that as you’re talking about this that I’m
thinking about as far as S.R.D.S. and Oxford’s both concerned is,
there is a whole business to business aspect to the newsletter
industry as well.
Bob Bly: 	I was just gonna say.
Julie McManus: 	Yeah.
Bob Bly: 	Let me speak to that. I want to, and add to what Clayton said. If
you’re gonna want to start in the newsletter thing, you’re gonna,
newsletter industry, you’re gonna have difficulty writing off the
bat for Phillips and Agora and the big people. That’s number one.
However, there’s a way to get inside them. One is to not
necessarily start with doing a magalog or a package for an
acquisition, but they might want you to do a lesser, a smaller piece
of copy. So, you might suggest that you do, like, a renewal or a
blanket, an advance or blanket renewal mailing or –
Clayton Makepeace: 	___ huge demand, Bob, for web copy too.
Bob Bly: 	I was just gonna say web copy –

-- 28 of 32 --

The Profit Center 	7_Q_and_A 	Page 29 of 32
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 29 of 32
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yeah.
Bob Bly: 	-- you can get in that way.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Short little e-mails, that kind of thing.
Bob Bly: 	Yeah, e-mail blasts. So, you can do that with the, even with the
big people. Then, there’s the second market that Clayton talked
about which is people who publish consumer newsletters but
they’re not the majors. But, there’s some companies out there that
they, you know, they have very good newsletters, some of them
have three to five thousand subscribers, some of them have tens of
thousands but they only publish three or four newsletters and
they’re not as bombarded by copywriters and they might be more
open to you.
And, the third, as Julie just said, is the business to business market.
And, here you can go to the big publishers. There’s a whole area
of selling business newsletters to executives and small businesses.
And, these newsletters typically charge forty-nine dollars a year to
five hundred dollars a year, depending on the, how specialized
their topic is. And, there’s very good money that can be made in
doing that and these people, there’s much less competition.
Julie McManus: 	Well, and since we know that a lot of people that are coming into
copywriting tend to be coming in on, say, a second leg in their
career, they came from very specialized backgrounds where you
could probably also find a lot of publications that speak
specifically to what you did, perhaps, in your first career. So, you
might actually be able to segway into writing or, let’s say, if you
were, you know, in printing or paper buying in your previous
lifetime, there’s a newsletter on, you know, paper buying.
Bob Bly: 	Julie, that’s such a great idea, cause when you contact them you
say, you know, if it’s a newsletter and that goes, you know, in the
printing industry and you say, “I know, you know, what, I know
the issues because I was a paper buyer for X Y Z or I was a
printing, you know, I was a pre-press operator or whatever,”
they’re much more interesting. They’re really, the business to
business newsletters are very interested in your specific knowledge
and experience of their industry and that’s a good way to get into
there.
Couple of more questions and then we’ll wrap cause we’re
1:15 p.m. I think we discussed this, or maybe we didn’t. But,

-- 29 of 32 --

The Profit Center 	7_Q_and_A 	Page 30 of 32
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 30 of 32
someone wants a clarification. The question is, what does exactly
does the term copy chief mean?
Clayton Makepeace: 	Okay. A copy chief is basically the boss. It’s used as a verb and
noun, okay. Copy chiefing is another writer who is essentially
reading and directing you. Right? So, you, the copy chief would
typically work with you to develop the theme of the package and
the major points that need to be covered in the package.
Bob Bly: 	Right.
Clayton Makepeace: 	And then would review each draft and would mark them up with
comments, preferably with comments, not with actual changes in
copy insertion, but saying, “You’re weak here, I don’t believe this.
This, you know, bad word choice. I’m getting bored. I deserve
combat pay for this, for reading this.” You know, a copy chief is
gonna push you to produce the very best copy you can possibly
produce.
The, and the act of doing that is called, we call copy chiefing.
They’re not, a mentor is someone who kind of takes you by the
hand and has a longer term relationship with you and kind of
moves you along. A copy chief can just be on one assignment, it’s
the person who’s driving the copy process and who gets to stay,
“Stick a fork in it, it’s done. This is great,” you know. And, gets
to give you all of the negative reinforcement you need to get up to
that point. So, that’s what copy chiefing is.
Bob Bly: 	We have time for, I think, one more question. And, this is from
Mark. He says, I think we’ve answered this but people are still
asking. “If you get the chance to write for a high paying client and
you blow it, the package bombed, is that it? Is that necessarily the
end of your relationship with them or will they give you a second
chance?
Clayton Makepeace: 	Somebody wasn’t on one of our calls.
Bob Bly: 	We kind of answered this?
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yeah we did. No, the, there’re a lot of things that go into the
success or failure of a package –
Bob Bly: 	And we went over it. If you go back and listen to the calls,
Clayton went over five or six factors that determine that.

-- 30 of 32 --

The Profit Center 	7_Q_and_A 	Page 31 of 32
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 31 of 32
Clayton Makepeace: 	Right, essentially, you know, the copy could be great, could be
brilliant copy, could be fantastic and just miss the market. Or, in
other words, it doesn’t resonate with readers, it’s a theme that
everyone thought would but it doesn’t. But, the very next, you
know, if the experience of working with you was a positive one
and if the copy itself was good solid, professional, well conceived
and executed copy, the publisher’s not probably gonna walk away
from ya. It would take a string of failures, of consistent failures
before you’d have to worry about that.
Timing in the market, there are a lot of other factors, the graphic
design might have been weak, list selection at smaller companies
where they don’t have a good list history could have been a
problem. There could be any number of things contributing to the
lack of success of a package. So, don’t panic. Everyone has
failures. I have failures. Even now after thirty some odd years I
have packages I really believe in that just didn’t perform and –
Julie McManus: 	Or packages where you write for a specific topical event and it
doesn’t happen or –
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yeah that’s, yeah –
Julie McManus: 	-- it hits right after it –
Clayton Makepeace: 	______ that call about timing. I don’t remember which cal it was
but I think it might have been three, but, yeah, we’ve done it
where, you know, where we talk about, for example, rising energy
prices and they’re gonna explode and they’re gonna go to a
hundred dollars a barrel for oil and everything. And, the
promotion just happens to drop –
Bob Bly: 	On the day oil goes down?
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yeah, on the day that oil starts a thirty percent decline that extends
over, about, two or three months. You know, you’re gonna get
your head handed to you in a situation like that. So, any number of
reasons, and clients who’ve worked with, you know, anybody
that’s worked in marketing for any period of time understands this
and they don’t shoot the copywriter. The key is, though, keep
plugging and, you know, watch out for those kinds of things and
avoid things that could reverse on you or things like that. And, do
your homework when it comes to the dominant emotions or your
marketplace, so you really feel like you can insert yourself into
your prospect’s shoes so you won’t have a misfire, in terms of the
resonant, the package resonating with the prospect.

-- 31 of 32 --

The Profit Center 	7_Q_and_A 	Page 32 of 32
Bob Bly, Clayton Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 32 of 32
Bob Bly: 	On that note, I want to thank Julie. It’s, you guys can imagine, it’s
a tremendous amount of work. We love having moved our
business here on the internet but it’s the technical things that
frustrate all of us. You know, you buy these products and systems
that say you can do this and that and they don’t work as easily as
you would think. Those of you listening to the call who have
internet marketing businesses, you know this.
And, Julie has done a tremendous amount of work. So, I want to
thank Julie and I, and of course, most of all, want to thank Clayton
who, again, as we explained to Peter why he’s doing this, we’re
lucky he is doing this and has shared, I think, again, I’ve been in
copywriting for twenty-five years, information and advice that has
never been available. And these ideas, I do believe, both in terms
of copywriting as well as marketing yourself as a copywriter, can
help you take your, either your direct response business, if you
have one, or a copywriting business to the next level. So, I want to
thank both of you guys.
Clayton Makepeace: 	And, I want to thank everybody that’s been on these calls. You
guys are great. I deeply appreciate your continued reading of The
Total Package. And, please, if these calls have been helpful to you
at all, please drop us a note to
feedback@makepeacetotalpackage.com. It’ll help us encourage
more people to join us in these calls and to help more people break
into this great business and help more of our clients grow their
companies faster. So, let us hear from you and I want to thank you
again from the depth of my heart for joining us on the.
[End of Audio]

-- 32 of 32 --
